metadata.title: Fable 5.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: this is Liam, in for Bear. Claude Fable-5 is the most capable AI model on earth right now — and The honest advice is: don't use it. Not because it's bad. Because it's expensive, it just moved out of flat-rate subscriptions, and 99% of people will burn through a month's budget in a day without even noticing. This is the guide on when to use it, when not to, and how to think about an AI that costs like a senior lawyer.

props.command: when should I actually use Claude Fable-5 — and when should I not?

beats.narration_text: First, the lineup. Claude ships in tiers: Haiku is fast and cheap. Sonnet is the solid middle. Opus is the big one. Then there's a separate naming track for the absolute top model — instead of a number, they give it a name. The current name is Fable-5. It's the smartest AI model by benchmark, better than everything else on reasoning, coding, and long document work. And it has a timeline problem: it launched in June included in paid plans, got briefly banned by the US government on June 12th, came back July 1st at fifty percent of weekly limits, and left subscriptions entirely on July 12th. From that point, Fable runs on usage billing.

artifactLines: Haiku: fastest, cheapest. Good for simple, repetitive tasks.
Sonnet: the reliable middle — best cost-to-quality ratio for daily work.
Opus: the big one. Very smart, formerly the top tier.
Fable-5 (Mythos level): smartest on earth. Now usage-billed, not in subscription.

beats.narration_text: A useful framing is the clearest way to think about this. Fable-5 is a senior lawyer with twenty years of experience — one thousand dollars an hour. Sonnet-5 is the capable intern at one hundred an hour. You don't pay the senior partner to review every quick contract. You pay them to define the international strategy, then hand the drafting to the intern. The intern can still read what the senior wrote and execute it well. The disaster scenario: a team of a hundred people, nobody trained on which model to use, everyone defaults to Fable for everything. The failure scenario: a million-dollar Anthropic invoice. Not fun.

artifactLines: Fable = senior partner ($1,000/hr): use for strategy, hard reasoning, complex plans.
Sonnet = capable intern ($100/hr): use for drafting, execution, routine tasks.
The intern reads the partner's plan and follows it reliably.
Rule: match model intelligence to task complexity — not to your anxiety.
props.sparkLine: Strategy gets Fable. Rest gets Sonnet.

beats.narration_text: The key insight: don't prompt Fable-5. That sounds counterintuitive, but here's what it means. The old habit — typing a long, detailed prompt into the top model — is not how you get value from Fable. Instead, you give it context and let it plan. Fable's advantage is reasoning, not text generation. You set the goal and the constraints, Fable figures out how to get there, then Sonnet executes the steps. One high-quality Fable turn at the start of a session is worth more than five expensive Fable turns doing routine work.

props.artifactHeading: How to get value from the top model
artifactLines: Wrong: type a detailed prompt into Fable, wait for a long output.
Right: give Fable the goal + constraints, let it write the plan.
Then: hand the plan to Sonnet for execution. Sonnet follows Fable reliably.
One Fable planning turn > five Fable generation turns.

beats.narration_text: Where Fable genuinely earns its cost. Interactive charts that need real logic behind them, not just visual formatting. Spreadsheets with complex dependencies — twelve tabs, real formulas, board-ready. Deep market research across hundreds of sources where the synthesis has to be accurate, not just plausible. Co-writing anything where you've uploaded everything about yourself and need the output to actually sound like you. Deck generation where the argument structure matters. The pattern: tasks where being wrong is costly, or where the output will be seen by people who can tell.

shot.slotNote: SLOT — fill media/B04.png (screenshot of a Fable-5 complex output — e.g. multi-tab spreadsheet or market research artifact in Claude)

beats.narration_text: Where Fable is pure waste. Summarizing a document you already understand. Reformatting something. Drafting a routine email. Generating three LinkedIn caption options. Answering a factual question with a known answer. These are Sonnet tasks — or even Haiku tasks. The failure mode is psychological: people reach for the smartest model because it feels safer. But Fable doesn't make simple tasks better. It makes them expensive. The discomfort of using a cheaper model on a simple task is almost always wrong intuition.

artifactLines: Summarizing a document you already understand → Haiku.
Drafting a routine email or reformatting text → Sonnet.
Generating caption options, quick Q&A → Sonnet.
The rule: if being wrong costs nothing, don't use Fable.

beats.narration_text: The real danger is rollout without training. The rollout scenario: you give your team of a hundred people access to Fable-5 with no guidance on which model to use. They default to the smartest-sounding option — that's human behavior. Nobody tracks it because they don't know there's anything to track. Usage billing means the invoice grows silently. This is not a hypothetical. It's what happens in most enterprise AI rollouts. The fix is cheap: before you give anyone Fable access, write a one-paragraph decision rule. When to use Fable. When to use Sonnet. Post it somewhere everyone sees it.

props.title: team-model-rule.md
props.code:
# Model decision rule — post this for your team Use Fable-5 when:
- The task requires multi-step reasoning or strategy
- Being wrong would cost time, money, or credibility
- You are planning something a cheaper model will execute Use Sonnet-5 for everything else:
- Drafts, summaries, formatting, routine email
- Any task where a near-miss is acceptable When in doubt: start with Sonnet.
If the output is wrong, escalate to Fable.

beats.narration_text: The important limit is what Fable-5 doesn't fix. It doesn't fix a bad brief. If you give it vague instructions, it produces a confident-sounding, well-structured version of vague. It doesn't fix missing context — if you don't tell it what your company does, it guesses. And it doesn't fix the habit of not reviewing outputs. Fable makes fewer mistakes than Sonnet. It does not make zero mistakes. The senior lawyer still needs a client who reads the contract.

shot.sparkLine: Smart model, dumb brief: still bad.
shot.slotNote: SLOT — fill media/B07.png (screenshot of a Fable-5 response where the model asks clarifying questions — illustrating that it still needs good context)

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Before your next big Fable session, run this prompt to figure out exactly which parts of your work actually warrant the top model — and which ones you've been overcharging yourself on.

props.command: I want a model-routing audit. Ask me about my 5 most common Claude tasks. For each one, tell me whether it should go to Fable-5, Sonnet-5, or Haiku — with one sentence of reasoning. Be strict. I probably overuse Fable.

beats.narration_text: Fable-5 is the best model on earth. Use it for the tasks where that actually matters. Nik Bear Brown.

props.title: Fable 5.
props.subline: Smartest model — match it to the task
